coredata.dc:title: A facile, fast and intelligent fluorescent sensing strategy for Sudan I detection based on perovskite quantum dots encoded hollow imprinting microspheres
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaolei
Chen, Ya
Yu, Lu
Xu, Zhixiang
Han, Zhonghui
He, Jinxing
coredata.dc:description: Due to the globalized supply of food and rapid development of the food industry, food contamination has become a leading cause of food safety problems. In this study, a fluorescent sensing system for the fast and sensitive analysis of Sudan I was developed based on hollow molecularly imprinted polymers and a fluorescent encoding technique by using CsPbX3 perovskite quantum dots as the fluorescent elements. The introduction of hollow structure provided a high loading capacity, faster encoding efficiency, and shorter detection time. After loading Sudan I, the fluorescent intensity was obviously quenched. Under the optimized detection conditions, this analysis method achieved good linearity in the range of 0.1–300 μg L−1, a limit detection of 0.05 μg L−1, and spiked recoveries of 94.91 %–105.36 % in food samples. The whole detection process can be completed within 10 min. Importantly, this work also provided a smartphone-assisted intelligent detection platform for the visual and real-time analysis of Sudan I.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor
Quantum dots
Hollow structure
Smartphone sensing
Sudan I

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of HMIPs of Sudan I
Establishment of the HMIPs fluorescent sensing system
Optimum of prepared conditions of HMIPs
Structural analysis of the HMIPs and HMIPs-CsPbX3 fluorescent encoded microspheres
Fluorescence properties of the HMIPs-CsPbX3 encoded microspheres
Establishment of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent sensor
Performance analysis of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system

ZR2021QC078, ZR2021MC187), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant NO. 32102061), Major Innovation Pilot Project of Integration of Science, Education and Industry of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Science) (Grant NO. 2022JBZ01-08).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of (a) ZIF-8; (b) HMIPs with ZIF-8 and (c) without ZIF-8; the HRTEM images of (d) HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres and the mapping images of (e) original HMIPs; (f) HMIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5; (g) HMIPs-CsPbBr3, and (h) HMIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres.

XRD patterns of (a) ZIF-8, MIPs@ZIF-8, NIPs@ZIF-8, HMIPs and HNIPs, (b) HMIPs, HMIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5, HMIPs-CsPbBr3, and HMIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres, (c) HNIPs, HNIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5, HNIPs-CsPbBr3, and HNIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres; (d) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of HMIPs, HMIPs-CsPbBr3, HNIPs, MIPs and NIPs.

Fluorescent spectra of (a) PQDs with different colors and (b) three fluorescent encoded microspheres; True-color fluorescent images under a 365 nm UV lamp of (c) HMIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 (blue), HMIPs-CsPbBr3 (green), HMIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres (red), and (d) MIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 (blue), MIPs-CsPbBr3 (green) and MIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of (a) HMIPs-CsPbBr3 and (b) HNIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescence sensors after the addition of different concentrations of Sudan I (0–500 µg/L); the insets in (a) and (b) are the corresponding linear curves; F 0/F value of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 against (c) Sudan I, structural analogs, amino acids, antibiotics and (d) some ions.
The preparation process of HMIPs-CsPbX3 fluorescent encoding microspheres.
The detection parameters of Sudan Ⅰ in different samples.
Comparison of the proposed sensor with other reported methods of Sudan Ⅰ detection.

row.entry: Tap water, river water, chili oil, chili powder, tomato paste
Eggs, duck eggs, tomato sauce, chili oil, chili powder
Eggs, duck eggs, tomato sauce, chili oil, chili powder

head.ce:title: A facile, fast and intelligent fluorescent sensing strategy for Sudan I detection based on perovskite quantum dots encoded hollow imprinting microspheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai an 271018 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province
College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai an 271018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the globalized supply of food and rapid development of the food industry, food contamination has become a leading cause of food safety problems. In this study, a fluorescent sensing system for the fast and sensitive analysis of Sudan I was developed based on hollow molecularly imprinted polymers and a fluorescent encoding technique by using CsPbX3 perovskite quantum dots as the fluorescent elements. The introduction of hollow structure provided a high loading capacity, faster encoding efficiency, and shorter detection time. After loading Sudan I, the fluorescent intensity was obviously quenched. Under the optimized detection conditions, this analysis method achieved good linearity in the range of 0.1–300 μg L−1, a limit detection of 0.05 μg L−1, and spiked recoveries of 94.91 %–105.36 % in food samples. The whole detection process can be completed within 10 min. Importantly, this work also provided a smartphone-assisted intelligent detection platform for the visual and real-time analysis of Sudan I.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Sudan I is a heterophilic azo dye used in the chemical industry as a colorant for oils, waxes, textiles, inks, as well as floor polish [1]. Because of the potential risk to humans and animals, it is classified as class 3 carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) [2]. Due to the low cost and bright color, Sudan I is used as an illegal pigment additive in the food products to improve the color of food products, yet, which has been banned according to the European Union (EU) [3]. Up to now, various methods, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), immunoanalysis, and gas chromatography mass spectrometry (GC–MS), have been proposed for the detection of Sudan I, which always suffer from the complex operation process, expensive instrument, time consumption and training personnel [4–6]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a rapid and effective method for Sudan I analysis.

Rapid testing technology driven by food safety has shown a rapid development trend, necessitating the specific recognition of analytes due to the complex nature of the food matrix [7]. Molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors, combining excellent selective binding for the target molecules and highly real-time fluorescent sensor systems with good sensitivity, have gained increasing attention as effective tools for rapid detection of contaminants (such as pesticides, antibiotics, and mycotoxin) in food sample [8,9].

During the fabrication of fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), the traditional strategy involves the use of optical quantum dots (QDs) directly incorporated in beads during synthesis, either by capped or by chemical grafting [10,11]. This limits the application of novel QDs due to compatibility issues with the polarity of MIPs reaction system and QDs [12]. And the long reaction time also could affect the fluorescent performance of QDs. The above problems can be addressed using optically encoded technique, in which the fluorescent encoded microspheres can be easily obtained by embedding QDs or other fluorescent materials into a polymer matrix, providing unique emission spectra. The advantages of flexible encoding with good compatibility since the preparation process of imprinted polymers and encoding process are separated [13]. In our previous study, we used the MIPs as the encoding matrix and novel QDs (CsPbX3 perovskite QDs, PQDs) as fluorescent elements to obtain CsPbX3@MIPs fluorescent microspheres. Then, a fluorescent sensing system for Sudan I detection was fabricated [14]. The stability of the original PQDs was promoted, and the high accuracy of the detection method from the specific adsorption of MIPs was confirmed. However, the structural nature of traditional MIPs, with most of the adsorption sites inside the MIPs, resulted in the low loading efficiency of PQDs and long detection time (almost 30 min), which could not meet the requirement of fast detection on site.

Hollow structures serve as an advanced material with an internal cavity and outer tunable shell, providing the abundant significant features of large specific surface area, high loading capacity, and fast mass transfer rate [15–17]. To enhance the adsorption features of bulk MIPs, it is an effective strategy by using hollow structures as core particles to construct hollow core–shell style imprinting polymers [18,19]. Although the wide application of hollow structures has been presented, the removal process of the inner core template remains a challenge, with the template commonly removed through higher temperature calcination or chemical etching methods, inducing a structurally imperfect and damaging effect on origin polymers [20,21].

ZIF-8, belong to the metal–organic framework materials, is noted for its extensive specific surface area, ease preparation process, and good thermal stability [22,23]. Importantly, ZIF-8 can be easily removed due to its instability under acidic conditions. This property makes ZIF-8 an ideal supporting material to achieve hollow core–shell structures. Liu et al. prepared a sensor through the aqueous polymerization of biomass carbon dots, ZIF-8, and MIPs. The addition of phenolic acid could disrupt the framework of ZIF-8. Under the same detection conditions, the established hollow structure sensor performed a 2-fold faster detection rate than the conventional sensor, thus realizing rapid detection [24]. Yang et al. used acetic acid to remove ZIF-8 to construct hollow-structured covalent organic frameworks (HCOFs) for use as solid phase extraction adsorbents, where the large pore size structure of HCOFs obtained by removing ZIF-8 resulted in an adsorption efficiency of HCOFs that was seven times higher than that of pristine COFs, and the adsorption equilibrium time was greatly reduced [25].

Based on the aforementioned considerations, the aim of this study was to integrate the fluorescence response of PQDs, the selective recognition and faster transfer rate (or recognition speed) of hollow MIPs (as HMIPs), and the large specific surface area of ZIF-8. This integration was intended to prepare novel hollow fluorescent molecularly imprinted encoded microspheres. Furthermore, a fluorescent system for the quantitative detection of Sudan I using both instrumental and smartphone-assisted intelligent detection platforms were established. The specific operation process of fluorescent encoded HMIPs microspheres is shown in Scheme 1 , which introduced ZIF-8 as a supporting material and used the instability of ZIF-8 under acidic conditions to prepare HMIPs. Subsequently, HMIPs and CsPbX3 PQDs were combined to prepare fluorescent encoded microspheres (HMIPs-CsPbX3).

PQDs with CsPbCl1.5Br1.5, CsPbBr3, and CsPbI2Br were acquired from Nanjing Mako Nano Technology Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Chemicals such as 2-methylimidazole, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), and divinylbenzene (DVB) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Additionally, Sudan I, Sudan II, Sudan III, Sudan IV, norfloxacin (NOR), tetracyline (TC), ofloxacin (OFX), and enrofloxacin (ENX) were also provided by this company. Methanol, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, acetonitrile, n-hexane, and glacial acetic acid were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Methacrylic acid (MAA) used in this study was obtained from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The experimental food samples, including chicken eggs, duck eggs, chili oil, tomato paste, and chili powder, were obtained from a local supermarket in Jinan, Shandong Province. AIBN and DVB should be purified before using, and other reagents were directly used without any purification process.

Fluorescence measurements were conducted using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-2700, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). UV–visible spectra were captured using a dual-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (TU-1901, Beijing Pulse General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Morphological evaluations were performed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy with field emission (HRTEM, JEM-F200, Tokyo, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-1400F, JEOL, Tokyo). The crystal structures of the polymers were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (Smartlab, Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo).

The fluorescence spectra were captured with an excitation wavelength at 365 nm, excitation and emission slit widths at 5 nm, and a photomultiplier voltage of 400 V. The emission wavelength was set in the range of 450–550 nm. The fluorescence intensity was calculated as the average of three independent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of HMIPs of Sudan I
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of ZIF-8 was shown in the supplementary material. The HMIPs for Sudan I was obtained using the following process. 100 mg of ZIF-8 were accurately weighted into 20 mL of acetonitrile, and sonicated for 10 min after the addition. Afterward, Sudan I (0.1 mmol, as template molecule), MAA (0.4 mmol, as functional monomer), EGDMA and DVB (1 mmol, 1 mmol, as cross-linker) and AIBN (20 mg, as initiator) were all added into the above solution. The reaction system was ultrasonicated for 5 min, sealed immediately after passing nitrogen for 15 min, and stirred continuously in a water bath under 60℃ for 24 h. Subsequently, the obtained polymers (MIPs@ZIF-8) were eluted with methanol/acetic acid (8:2, v/v) to remove the template and ZIF-8. The resulting particles were washed with methanol to neutralize and dried in an oven at 60℃ to a constant weight.

As a control, hollow non-imprinted polymers (HNIPs) were synthesized using the same procedure but without the Sudan I addition. Meanwhile, to confirm the advantages of hollow structures, the traditional solid MIPs/NIPs (MIPs/NIPs) were prepared using the same process with the absence of ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the HMIPs fluorescent sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent encoded microspheres were first prepared by mixing the fluorescent elements and encoding matrix, in which CsPbX3 PQDs with different colors (CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 PQDs, CsPbBr3 PQDs, and CsPbI2Br PQDs) were separately selected as the fluorescent elements under the HMIPs as the encoded matrix. First, a 2 mL centrifuge tube was filled with 1 mL of hexane and 100 μL of a 10 mg mL−1 CsPbX3 PQD solution. Next, 10 mg of HMIPs was added to the system, sonicated to achieve homogeneous dispersion, followed by shaken on a shaker for 5 min. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the supernatant. The residue was then washed with n-hexane for three times until no fluorescence signals of the PQDs were detected in the supernatant. Finally, the HMIPs-CsPbX3 microspheres with three different colors (HMIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5, HMIPs-CsPbBr3, and HMIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres) were obtained by placing the samples under room temperature until a constant weight was reached. As a control, the MIPs/NIPs were also encoded with three different colored PQDs using the same encoding method to prepare the MIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5, MIPs-CsPbBr3, and MIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres.

To established the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescence probe for the fluorescence spectrophotometer detection, the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system with concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1 was firstly obtained. Then, different concentrations of Sudan I/n-hexane solution were added to the above pre-prepared fluorescent sensing system to achieve final concentrations of Sudan I of 0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, and 500 µg/L. After shaken for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

To establish the smartphone-assisted intelligent detection platform, the circular filter paper pieces of the same size were first cut, and then put into HMIPs-CsPbBr3/n-hexane solution for 5 min and dried under room temperature. The above filter paper were added with different concentrations of Sudan I solution dropwise (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 µg/L, and 1, 2, 5, 10 mg L−1). After incubation for 10 min, the fluorescent color were recorded under the UV lamp at 365 nm and the photos were captured by a smartphone. Then the photo color was shifted to the red–green–blue (RGB) values using color recognition application to eliminate the influence of human factors and analyte the content of Sudan I. The smartphone-assisted intelligent detection process was shown in Fig. S1.
During the measurement, each experiment was independently repeated at least three times, and the results were presented as the mean ± relative standard deviation to ensure accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum of prepared conditions of HMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: During the synthesis of HMIPs, Sudan I, MAA, and EGDMA and DVB were selected as template, functional monomer, and cross-linker, respectively. Generally, the molar ratios between template, functional monomer and cross-linker could affect the imprinted effect of imprinted polymers and improve the accuracy of fluorescent imprinted sensor. To confirm the optimized ratio, the effect of various ratios of template and functional monomer (Sudan I:MAA, 1:2, 1:4, 1:10, 1:20, and 1:40) on adsorption capacity and imprinted factor (IF) value were first evaluated when the constant quantity of ZIF-8 was 100 mg. As shown in Table S1, the highest imprinted effect with molar ratio of 1:4 was found. At a low molar ratio with 1:2, the low template-functional monomer mixture and less imprinted sites were formed, while the molar ratio at 1:10, 1:20, and 1:40 produced a high non-specific adsorption, resulting a low imprinted effect [26]. Second, different molar ratios of templates, functional monomer and cross-linker (Sudan I:MAA:EGDMA + DVB) were also studied. From Table S2, we found the optimized compositions at 1:4:20, and the amount increase of cross-linker could result in the excessive cross-linker domains providing a poor access to the template. Therefore, the ratio of Sudan I:MAA:EGDMA + DVB was selected 1:4:20 to prepare the HMIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of the HMIPs and HMIPs-CsPbX3 fluorescent encoded microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: TEM analysis was used to observe the internal structures of the ZIF-8 and HMIPs, and HNIPs, with the results shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. S2. As indicated in Fig. 1(a), the obtained ZIF-8 exhibited a rhombic dodecahedral structure, which was consistent with the literature reports [27]. During the preparation of the HMIPs/HNIPs, ZIF-8 was first used as a core to form the core–shell-type structure polymers. Due to its structural instability under acidic conditions, the ZIF-8 structure could directly disintegrate and be gently removed during elution of the template, forming a hollow structure at the original ZIF-8 position [28,29]. Compared with the core–shell structure shown in Fig. 1(b) and Fig. S2(a), the hollow shape of the HMIPs/HNIPs was obviously observed (Fig. 1(c) and Fig. S2(b)), indicating the successful preparation of the HMIPs and HNIPs.

After encoding the matrix HMIPs/HNIPs, the presence of PQDs could be detected in the outer shell layer of the fluorescent encoded microspheres (Fig. 1(d) and Fig. S2(c)), and loaded PQDs were almost exclusively present in the whole shell layer. In addition, the microspheres still maintained their original hollow structure after encoding. To further study the effective loading of CsPbX3 PQDs, the HMIPs-CsPbX3 fluorescent encoded microspheres were subjected to mapping analysis of the characteristic elements (Fig. 1(e)–(h)). Meanwhile, the characteristic elements of PQDs, namely Cs, Pb, Cl, Br, and I, were shown within the entire encoded microspheres (Fig. 1(f)–(h)). However, these elements were absent in the original HMIPs (Fig. 1(e)), providing direct evidence of the successful preparation of the encoded microspheres.

XRD analysis was also conducted to further confirm the successful preparation of the HMIPs, HNIPs, HMIPs-CsPbX3 and HNIPs-CsPbX3 microspheres. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the characteristic diffraction peaks of the prepared ZIF-8 agreed well with the XRD results of the ZIF-8 simulations obtained from the Cambridge Structure Data Center (CCDC 602542) [29,30]. Before removal the ZIF-8, ZIF-8 characteristic diffraction peaks were obviously observed, indicating that ZIF-8 was successfully incorporated into the material. By contrast, the absence of the ZIF-8 distinct diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns of the HMIPs and HNIPs obtained after elution, exhibiting the entirely removal of ZIF-8. The XRD patterns of the HMIPs-CsPbX3 and HNIPs-CsPbX3 microspheres with three different colors are also shown in Fig. 2(b) and Fig. 2(c), and the XRD patterns after the combination of the HMIPs/HNIPs and three PQDs all demonstrated diffraction peaks specific to the three PQDs, which further indicated the successful encoding of the PQDs and HMIPs/HNIPs.

Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were used to analyze the adsorption performance of the obtained polymers, which confirmed the advantages of adsorption performance of the hollow structure. From Fig. 2(d), we could see that the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of five samples exhibited obvious hysteresis loops in the high-pressure region and belonged to type IV. The average pore size of the HMIPs, HNIPs, MIPs, and NIPs microspheres were 16.9 nm, 6.7 nm, 12.2 nm and 5.1 nm, respectively. The result suggested the existence of a mesoporous structure (2–50 nm). According to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) theory, the HMIPs exhibited a notably higher specific surface area of 36.9 m2 g−1, while the specific surface areas of HNIPs, MIPs, and NIPs were 20.2 m2 g−1, 2.8 m2 g−1 and 2.7 m2 g−1. This result gave a directly evidence that the HMIPs could provide a better adsorption capacity to adsorb more template molecules and PQDs [31]. Meanwhile, a reduction specific surface area of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres with 28.6 m2 g−1 was found, suggesting amounts of PQDs could be captured into the interior of the HMIPs microspheres through non-covalent interaction, such as electrostatic and hydrophobic interaction [13,31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of the HMIPs-CsPbX3 encoded microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: Three different colored CsPbX3 PQDs were used as fluorescent materials and encoded with HMIPs substrates. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), the fluorescence emission spectra of pure PQDs and the corresponding encoded microspheres exhibited the extremely similar feature from the shape of emission spectra and maximum emission wavelength. When exposed to a UV lamp at 365 nm (see Fig. 3(c) and (d)), the pure PQDs and encoded microspheres all exhibited the true color of CsPbX3 PQDs; however, the fluorescent brightness of the MIPs microspheres was significantly lower than that of the hollow encoded microspheres. Meanwhile, the quantum yield of the HMIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 microspheres was 44.8 %, that of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres was 76.3 %, and that of the HMIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres was 62.6 %. The quantum yield of the MIPs-CsPbCl1.5Br1.5 microspheres was 38.3 %, the MIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres had a quantum yield of 61.9 %, and the MIPs-CsPbI2Br microspheres had a quantum yield of 51.9 %. Compared with conventional solid MIPs, the unique hollow structure of the HMIPs could increase the encoding capacity and enhance the quantum yield of encoded microspheres. The higher quantum yield predicted the more vigorous fluorescence intensity of the encoded microspheres, which facilitated subsequent fluorescence detection of the template molecules [33].

In the following experiments, the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent encoded microspheres were employed as an example to validate the encoding conditions, stability, and the establishment of fluorescent sensing system for Sudan I detection.
During the encoding process, the amount of PQDs and the encoding time served as important factors related to the fluorescence intensity provided by the encoded microspheres.

The relationship between PQDs addition amount and the fluorescence intensity was first investigated (see Fig. S3(a)). When the amount of CsPbBr3 PQDs ranged from 0–50 µL, the fluorescent intensity of both HMIPs and HNIPs microspheres performed highly increased, yet, the fluorescence intensity of the HMIPs reached its maximum with 100 µL of PQDs (50 µL for HNIPs) due to the presence of imprinting sites in the imprinted polymer that were lacking in the non-imprinted polymer, enabling the imprinted polymer to be loaded with more PQDs. Similar to a previous study, when the incorporation of PQDs exceeded the maximum loading amount, the fluorescence intensity of the microspheres exhibited a stronger decreased phenomenon, attributing to the self-quenching effect of PQDs [34]. In addition, good linearity between the fluorescent intensity of imprinted encoded microspheres and the amount of PQDs (0–100 µL) was obtained with R2 = 0.992 (Fig. S3(b)), indicating that fluorescence intensity of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres is easy prediction and precise control. In the subsequent experiments, the optimized addition amount of CsPbBr3 PQDs was 100 µL.

Next, the effect of encoding time on the fluorescence intensity was evaluated. In this study, the HMIPs were directly used as the encoding matrix, in which the specific hollow structure provided a higher specific surface area and the high density of imprinting sites on the outside of the imprinted shell layer. We hypothesized that the hollow imprinted polymer had a higher encoding efficiency and shorter time than the solid imprinted matrix MIPs. As shown in Fig. S3(c), the fluorescence intensity reached 53.0 % of the maximum value when the encoding time was 1 min, and the maximum value was reached at 5 min. The encoding time continued to be prolonged and did not increase significantly, and 5 min was chosen as the optimal encoding time, which is much shorter than that using the bulk MIPs as encoding matrix in our previous study (20 min). This indicated that hollow structures could enhance the encoding efficiency and shorten the encoding time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent sensor

ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the stability of fluorescent property of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres, photo stability, storage stability, performance against the polar solvent, and chemical stability were studied, as shown in supplementary material. Based on the result, the encapsulation of CsPbBr3 PQDs into HMIPs matrix significantly improved the stability of CsPbBr3 PQDs, confirming the accuracy of detection method during the practical application.

The concentration of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres was an essential factor that affected the detection performance of the fluorescent sensors. 200 μg L−1 Sudan I was selected as the analyte to investigate the fluorescent response (F 0 /F) between Sudan I and different concentrations of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres (0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0mg mL−1). The result was shown in Fig. S6(a). Although the fluorescent response of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres enhanced with the increasing of microspheres concentration, yet, the quenching effect was achieved the highest at a level of 1.0 mg mL−1, followed by a decrease. Then 1.0 mg mL−1 of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres were selected to establish the fluorescent probe.

The response time served as another essential factor affecting the detection efficiency. When the concentration of Sudan I was 200 μg L−1, the fluorescence response (F0/F) under different adsorption time (1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, and 30 min) was studied at room temperature. As depicted in Fig. S6(b), the fluorescence response significantly increased from 1 to 10 min and were maintained at almost equilibrium. In order to reduce the analysis time, 10 min was selected in the real analysis process. Notably, the response time was significantly shorter than that obtained in our previous study using a solid imprinted encoding fluorescent system (30 min), which was attributed to the hollow structure of the imprinting polymers, significantly improving the mass transfer rates. Our observation also revealed that the quenching effect of imprinted encoded microspheres was much greater than that of the corresponding non-imprinted CsPbBr3 microspheres, leading from the exactly recognition sites existing within the imprinting layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence intensity changes of the fluorescent system after adding different concentrations (0–500 µg/L) of Sudan I solution were determined under optimal detection conditions. In our previous study, we confirmed that the quenching mechanism of MIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres under loading Sudan I was the internal filtration effect (IFE), in which the CsPbBr3 PQDs within MIPs and Sudan I acted as energy donors and energy acceptor, respectively [14]. During the reaction process, the energy from the CsPbBr3 PQDs as donors was absorbed by the Sudan I as acceptors, triggering the IFE, which then led to the fluorescent quenching effect. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b), upon the addition of Sudan I, both the established HMIPs-CsPbBr3 and HNIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent systems were all performed the fluorescent quenching effect. At the same concentration, due to the existence of imprinting sites within the imprinting layer, the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system exhibited a much greater fluorescent quenching effect (F0/F = 6.52) compared to the HNIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent probe (F0/F = 1.79). Based on the literature, the correlation between concentration of Sudan I and fluorescence quenching efficiency can be described using the Stern-Volmer equation (1) [35]:
(1)
F 0
/ F =
K SV
C
Sudan I
+ 1

Where, F0 and F mean the fluorescent intensity of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system in the absence and presence of Sudan I; the quenching coefficient is denoted as K SV; and the concentration of Sudan I is represented by C Sudan I.

As shown in the inset of Fig. 4(a), an excellent linear correlation (R2 = 0.998) between the fluorescent quenching degree and the concentration of Sudan I was obtained at the concentration ranged from 0.1 μg L−1 to 300 μg L−1, with an imprinting factor of 4.80. The limit of detection (LOD) of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 sensor was determined as 0.05 μg L−1.

In order to study the specific recognition of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system, Sudan I, Sudan II, Sudan III, and Sudan IV were selected as the structural analogs, and the structures were shown in Fig. S7. From the Fig. 4(c) and Fig. S8(a), only Sudan I achieved the obviously fluorescent quenching efficiency, while just negligibly fluorescence change was observed upon the addition of three other Sudan dyes with F0/F ranging from 1.05 to 1.07, even though the similar UV absorption spectra for Sudan I, Sudan II, Sudan III, and Sudan IV (see Fig. S8(c)). The slight fluorescence quenching only depended on nonspecific adsorption.

To evaluate the anti-interference ability, various co-existed compounds in food samples were further introduced into the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system, including the different common antibiotics, amino acids, sugars, and ions. Fig. 4(c) and (d) illustrates that the addition of other co-existed compounds could not result in a notable fluorescence quenching effect, and fluorescent quenching of the established fluorescent system was negligible.

These above results indicated the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability, which could avoid the interference of complex food matrices on the detection system.

To assess the practical applicability in real samples, the developed HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system was utilized for the detection of Sudan I in eggs, duck eggs, tomato sauce, chili powder, and chili oils. The specific process of sample preparation was detailed in the supplementary material, and the recovery experiments were conducted at four spiked concentrations with 0.5, 10, 50, and 100 μg L−1 in various samples to assess accuracy and precision. As shown in Table 1 , the spiked recoveries of the HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system were ranged from 94.91 % to 105.36 %, and the RSD values were all less than 6.28 %, which met the general requirements for quantitative analysis. The results showed that the developed fluorescent system had excellent intra-day (within 1 day) and inter-day (within 3 days) precision, with good precision in detection. To further confirm the accuracy, the obtained results were validated through the traditional HPLC method. The analytical process of HPLC was shown in supplementary material. As shown in Table S3, no Sudan I reside was detected in blank samples, which is consistent with the developed fluorescent analytical method. More importantly, the sensitivity of fluorescent sensor was much higher than that of HPLC method since the spiked concentration with 0.5 µg/L was lower than the limit of detection of HPLC method.

Table 2 lists the parameters of the some currently reported methods for detecting Sudan I. It is obviously observed that the fluorescent system developed in this study offered a shorter detection time, lower detection limit, and higher accuracy than other reported methods, and this fluorescent sensor could be applied to a broader range of samples. Therefore, the developed HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent system provided more selectivity to achieve high-accuracy quantitative detection of Sudan I in real samples.

Although the above fluorescent detection by fluorescence spectrophotometer performed a high accuracy and sensitivity analysis strategy, it still requires the expensive instrument fluorescence spectrophotometer. Then a smartphone-assisted intelligent platform was further developed. As shown in Fig. S9(a), the fluorescence color of the fluorescent sensing under the UV lamp demonstrated a more noticeable change from bright to dark as the concentration of Sudan I increased. Smartphone analysis showed that the concentration of Sudan I had an excellent linear relationship with the G/R value in the concentration range of 0–200 μg L−1 (R2 = 0.993), as shown in Fig. S9(b–c).

To evaluate the practical application of smartphone assisted sensing platform, the actual experiments by spiked concentrations with 50, 100, 150 µg/L in the five samples. To confirm the accuracy, ten G/R values at different area on the same filter paper were captured, and calculated the average value for the determination. As shown in Table S4, the favorable recoveries (94.36–104.32 %) with RSD < 6.42 % were obtained. This is comparable to the results obtained from the HMIPs-CsPbX3 fluorescent sensor and HPLC method. The above results verified that this smartphone-assisted sensing platform has a promising application in analyzing Sudan I in food products.

In summary, the HMIPs-CsPbX3 fluorescent imprinted microspheres with unique optical properties of PQDs have been successfully prepared based on the optical encoding technique. Benefiting from the excellent fluorescent performance and specific adsorption of HMIPs-CsPbX3 microspheres, two modes analytical strategies, including fluorescence spectrophotometer method and smartphone assisted-intelligent platform, have been established and applied for the detection of Sudan I in complex food matrices. Then, this experiment could provide a faster, simpler, and more sensitive platform for identifying Sudan I, which would further be expanded the application of fluorescent encoded microspheres in the field of analysis and detection.

Xiaolei Zhao: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Ya Chen: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Lu Yu: Supervision, Investigation. Zhixiang Xu: Validation, Formal analysis. Zhonghui Han: Project administration. Jinxing He: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank LetPub (www.letpub.com.cn) for its linguistic assistance during the preparation of this manuscript.

This work was supported by Key R&D Program of Shandong Province, China (Grant NO. 2023CXGC010712), the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (Grant NO. ZR2021QC078, ZR2021MC187), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant NO. 32102061), Major Innovation Pilot Project of Integration of Science, Education and Industry of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Science) (Grant NO. 2022JBZ01-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of ZIF-8; Stability evaluation; Sample preparation; HPLC analytical process; Figures showing the schematic diagram of smartphone integrated sensing platform to detect Sudan I in Fig. S1; the TEM images of HNIPs and HNIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres in Fig. S2; The optimization of encoding conditions in Fig. S3; Stability evaluation of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 microspheres in Fig. S4; Leaking percentages of CsPbBr3 PQDs in hexane solution with increasing washing times in Fig. S5; Optimization of detection conditions in Fig. S6; Molecular structure of Sudan Ⅰ–Ⅳ in Fig. S7; The fluorescence spectra of HMIPs-CsPbBr3 fluorescent sensor under the addition of 200 µg/L of Sudan I, Sudan II, Sudan III, and Sudan IV, fluorescent spectrograms and UV absorption spectra of Sudan I, Sudan II, Sudan III, and Sudan IV in Fig. S8; The fluorescent photographs of fluorescent sensing under 365 nm UV irradiation, plot of G/B value versus Sudan I concentration, and the linear relationship between G/B value and Sudan I concentration (0–200 μg L−1) in Fig. S9; Tables showing the optimization of the ratio of Sudan I and MAA in Table S1; The optimization of the ratio of Sudan I, MAA, and EGDMA + DVB in Table S2; The detection of Sudan Ⅰ using the HPLC method in different samples in Table S3; The detection of Sudan I in real samples by the developed smartphone-based sensing method in Table S4. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112800.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
